meta.title: Rocket Market Map 2026 / 2027-2030
meta.subtitle: Interactive strategic dashboard for launch supply, technology paths, competitive economics and capital structure across the U.S., Europe and China.
meta.scope_note: Coverage is a curated strategic market map of orbital launch supply nodes in the U.S., Europe and China. It emphasizes programs that either deliver orbital launch today or matter for 2027-2030 competitive structure. It is not a census of every micro-launch startup.
methodology: MECE split 1 — Time status: every node belongs to exactly one bucket: Live 2026 supply, 2027-2030 option, or Distressed / paused.
MECE split 2 — Ownership: each node is classified as exactly one of State system, State-backed commercial, Industrial JV / incumbent, Public company, or Private company.

MECE split 3 — Technology: reusability and propellant each use one exclusive category per node. Large national families are modeled as a single fleet node only when splitting sub-variants would double count supply.
2026 deliverable kg = conservative full-year base-case supply likely to be commercially or institutionally deliverable in 2026. Development programs usually score zero here.
2030 potential kg = directional base-case scenario if the program reaches a plausible 2030 cadence. These are scenario inputs for strategy, not management guidance or audited forecasts.
Cost score is a strategic ranking, not a quoted price sheet. It rewards reusable depth, flight cadence, manufacturability and mission-market fit more than brochure payload alone.

insights.title: 2026 is still a current-supply game
insights.body: SpaceX dominates delivered launch supply today; China’s state system is the only other ecosystem with real recurring thickness; Europe is rebuilding sovereign access rather than winning on headline $/kg.

insights.title: 2027-2030 is a reusable-medium/heavy race
insights.body: The structural battleground is no longer tiny rockets. It is whether medium/heavy reusable vehicles can turn first flight into repeat flight into factory cadence.

insights.title: Capital matters almost as much as propulsion
insights.body: Launch is brutally capital-intensive. Programs with founder balance sheets, public-market access, or state-backed industry funds have a much longer runway to absorb failures and iteration cycles.

insights.title: The right question is not just who can fly
insights.body: The right question is who can repeatedly deliver launch, at a tolerable unit cost, with enough cadence and enough capital to survive the ugly middle years.

nodes.route_summary: Reusable first stage, mature booster reuse, high-rate factory and launch cadence.
nodes.current_reality: The baseline industrial launch system in 2026. It is already delivering launch at aviation-like cadence compared with everyone else.
nodes.cost_thesis: Still the benchmark on delivered cost because reuse depth, refurbishment learning curve, internal Starlink demand and launch cadence reinforce each other.
nodes.funding: Historically raised external capital; current financing mostly secondary transactions rather than classic venture rounds.
nodes.investors: Historical disclosed investors include Google and Fidelity; deep secondary-market demand.

nodes.route_summary: Reusable side boosters and center-core family commonality with Falcon 9.
nodes.current_reality: Niche heavy-lift workhorse for missions that need more throw than Falcon 9 but not a full Starship-class vehicle.
nodes.cost_thesis: Competitive for specific heavy missions, but long-term economics likely get eclipsed by Starship if Starship becomes operational at scale.
nodes.funding: See SpaceX company financing context.
nodes.investors: See SpaceX company financing context.

nodes.route_summary: Full-stack methane reusability with on-orbit refueling ambition and very large fairing volume.
nodes.current_reality: Immense option value but not yet a dependable commercial supply source in the conservative 2026 base case.
nodes.cost_thesis: If rapid full reuse and turn-around are solved, it can rewrite the global marginal cost curve rather than just compete inside it.
nodes.funding: See SpaceX company financing context.
nodes.investors: See SpaceX company financing context.

nodes.architecture_class: Reusable first stage + hydrogen upper stage
nodes.route_summary: Methane BE-4 booster with reusable first stage and hydrogen upper stage for high-energy missions.
nodes.current_reality: Now real, no longer a perpetual future promise, but still early in the cadence ramp versus Falcon-class operations.
nodes.cost_thesis: Can become a credible heavy reusable competitor if flight rate climbs; without cadence, fixed costs stay heavy.
nodes.funding: Jeff Bezos has said he sells about $1B of Amazon stock per year to fund Blue Origin.
nodes.investors: Founder-funded by Jeff Bezos.

nodes.architecture_class: Methane booster + hydrogen upper stage; solid-boosted variants
nodes.route_summary: High-performance methane/hydrogen architecture optimized for government and heavy commercial work, but without current operational reuse.
nodes.current_reality: Strong backlog and strong strategic relevance, but the 2026 anomaly pause lowers short-term schedule certainty.
nodes.cost_thesis: Reliable mission assurance matters, yet structurally it is unlikely to beat high-frequency reusable systems on long-run cost per kg.
nodes.funding: Not venture-backed in the normal sense; Boeing/Lockheed JV with government-heavy backlog.
nodes.investors: Boeing, Lockheed Martin.

nodes.route_summary: Dedicated small-launch vehicle with high cadence and strong operational discipline rather than lowest possible $/kg.
nodes.current_reality: Arguably the strongest pure-play small-launch franchise outside SpaceX’s rideshare gravity well.
nodes.cost_thesis: Electron wins on schedule control and dedicated access, not on lowest marginal cost; Neutron matters for Rocket Lab’s next step in economics.
nodes.valuation: Public company (market value fluctuates with share price)
nodes.funding: Public equity access plus operating cash generation from launch and space systems.
nodes.investors: Public shareholders.

nodes.route_summary: Medium reusable rocket sized for constellation deployment, national security and cargo missions.
nodes.current_reality: Important strategic option, but delayed to late 2026 after a tank test failure so it is not counted in conservative 2026 live supply.
nodes.cost_thesis: Among the clearest non-SpaceX medium-class reusable cost threats if Rocket Lab converts development discipline into cadence.
nodes.valuation: Public company (vehicle option embedded in Rocket Lab market cap)
nodes.funding: Funded through public company balance sheet and capital markets.
nodes.investors: Public shareholders.

nodes.route_summary: Carbon-composite small launcher with responsive-space positioning.
nodes.current_reality: A real launcher business, but still working through reliability perception and scale-up in a crowded small-launch market.
nodes.cost_thesis: Useful for responsive and sovereign niches; structurally less advantaged than reusable medium launchers on long-run $/kg.
nodes.funding: $175M Series D in 2024 at >$2B, then $868M IPO in 2025.
nodes.investors: Northrop Grumman, RPM Ventures, AE Industrial Partners and public shareholders.

nodes.route_summary: Firefly + Northrop medium launcher combining Alpha-derived propulsion and Antares heritage.
nodes.current_reality: Strategically significant because it moves Firefly up the value chain from small launch to a much more relevant market segment.
nodes.cost_thesis: If it enters service on time, it could become a credible mid-market alternative where national security diversification matters.
nodes.funding: Supported by Northrop partnership and Firefly public-market access.
nodes.investors: Northrop Grumman and public shareholders.

nodes.route_summary: Reusable methane launcher aimed at the constellation and large-payload market; no longer centered on maximal 3D-printing novelty.
nodes.current_reality: A meaningful backlog exists, but until first flight and production ramp happen, Terran R is still option value rather than dependable supply.
nodes.cost_thesis: The architecture makes sense economically; the real question is execution speed and capital durability.
nodes.valuation: $4.2B last widely cited private valuation mark (2021); newer capital terms not fully public
nodes.funding: $650M round in 2021; Eric Schmidt-backed recapitalization later reported but not fully disclosed.
nodes.investors: Historical investors include Fidelity, Baillie Gifford, Mark Cuban; later strategic backing from Eric Schmidt.

nodes.route_summary: One of the purest full-and-rapid-reuse plays outside SpaceX, including a reusable upper stage with metallic heat shield.
nodes.current_reality: Technically ambitious and strategically interesting, but still pre-revenue launch option value.
nodes.cost_thesis: If upper-stage rapid reuse works, the cost curve could be extraordinary; if not, the architecture loses its core edge.
nodes.funding: $260M Series C in 2025; then $510M Series D plus $100M debt facility; total capital raised approached $1B.
nodes.investors: US Innovative Technology Fund, Breakthrough Energy Ventures, Glade Brook, Point72 Ventures, Woven, Y Combinator and others.

nodes.route_summary: Europe’s main sovereign launcher, optimized for mission assurance and autonomy rather than low marginal cost.
nodes.current_reality: A real heavy launcher, back in service, and central to Europe’s independent access-to-space story.
nodes.cost_thesis: Strategically indispensable, but hard to see it becoming the global cost leader versus high-frequency reusable systems.
nodes.valuation: ArianeGroup is a 50/50 Airbus-Safran industrial joint venture
nodes.funding: Industrial incumbent backed by European institutional demand rather than venture capital.
nodes.investors: Airbus, Safran.

nodes.route_summary: Solid-powered small/medium launcher aimed at European sovereign and science payloads.
nodes.current_reality: Important to Europe’s launcher stack after return to flight, but not a global cost-outlier.
nodes.cost_thesis: Solid launchers remain useful for responsive and sovereign niches, but usually lose on structural cost to reusable liquids over time.
nodes.funding: Public-market and institutional European launcher support.
nodes.investors: Public shareholders; Leonardo retained a strategic stake after placement.

nodes.route_summary: European small launcher using LOX/propane and in-house engines, optimized for dedicated smallsat access.
nodes.current_reality: One of Europe’s most important launch startups, but still post-testflight rather than mature supply.
nodes.cost_thesis: Can matter for sovereign access and dedicated missions; unlikely by itself to reset global launch pricing.
nodes.valuation: ~$1B private valuation commonly cited around recent rounds
nodes.funding: >€400M raised; €150M Eldridge financing in 2025.
nodes.investors: 7-Industries, Bayern Kapital, Earlybird, HV Capital, Lakestar, Porsche SE, UVC, Vsquared, Eldridge.

nodes.architecture_class: Small launcher with recovered first stage
nodes.route_summary: Spanish small launcher with ocean-recovered first stage and strong industrialization ambition.
nodes.current_reality: Still pre-scale orbital supply, but one of Europe’s best-capitalized launcher startups and unusually explicit about factory/output ambitions.
nodes.cost_thesis: Could become a credible regional operator; likely more important as a sovereign and regional option than as a global cost killer.
nodes.funding: >€350M total after March 2026 Series C.
nodes.investors: Mitsubishi Electric, CDTI/INNVIERTE, COFIDES, Nazca and prior Spanish backers.

nodes.architecture_class: Mini launcher with reusable and expendable variants
nodes.route_summary: ArianeGroup-backed partially reusable European mini-launcher using bio-methane and LOX.
nodes.current_reality: Strategically important because it is Europe’s most serious incumbent-backed reusable launcher attempt.
nodes.cost_thesis: The economics depend on whether ArianeGroup can turn incumbent industrial depth into startup-like execution speed.
nodes.valuation: No standalone public valuation; ArianeGroup subsidiary
nodes.funding: Backed inside ArianeGroup / European institutional ecosystem.
nodes.investors: ArianeGroup.

nodes.route_summary: Small launcher using staged-combustion RP-1/LOX engines and an orbital transfer stage.
nodes.current_reality: Still a development-stage European contender rather than dependable launch supply.
nodes.cost_thesis: Potentially cost-competitive among European microlaunchers, but not obviously disruptive against reusable mid-lift systems globally.
nodes.funding: Privately funded with industrial/PE backing.
nodes.investors: Backers reported to include KKR, OHB and Apollo Capital Partners.

nodes.route_summary: Bio-propane micro-launch concept with future-reuse aspirations.
nodes.current_reality: Entered administration in February 2026, so it should not be treated as dependable future capacity today.
nodes.cost_thesis: In the current state, there is no investable launch-supply thesis until restructuring or asset sale clarifies the path forward.
nodes.valuation: About $220M after 2022 fundraising (reported), but now distressed
nodes.funding: Mix of grants and private equity prior to administration.
nodes.investors: Included UK government-related backing and Scottish National Investment Bank; now in administration.

nodes.vehicle: Long March operational fleet (ex. reusable pilots)
nodes.route_summary: China’s state launch backbone spanning multiple expendable liquid and solid families with rising cadence and constellation demand.
nodes.current_reality: The second-deepest launch ecosystem in the world after the U.S. when measured by recurring annual flight tempo.
nodes.cost_thesis: Powerful on throughput and state-backed demand capture, but still behind the best reusable commercial systems on pure structural cost efficiency.
nodes.funding: State budget, industrial capex and national program support.
nodes.investors: State-funded.

nodes.route_summary: State-backed methane reusable first-stage program, China’s main internal answer to the reusable medium-heavy market shift.
nodes.current_reality: Key technical signal but still not counted as dependable 2026 supply after its first recovery attempt failed.
nodes.cost_thesis: If scaled, it can compress the gap between China’s state system and commercial reusable challengers.
nodes.funding: State-funded program.
nodes.investors: State-funded.

nodes.route_summary: Fast-response commercial solid launcher positioned for constellation deployment and replenishment.
nodes.current_reality: A real commercial supply node inside China’s state-backed ecosystem.
nodes.cost_thesis: Useful tactical launcher, but long-run global cost leadership is unlikely without reuse.
nodes.valuation: State-backed commercial entity; private valuation not central
nodes.funding: Backed through the CASC ecosystem.
nodes.investors: State-backed commercial platform.

nodes.route_summary: Commercial methane launcher already in service; important because it validates engine, tanks and supply chain ahead of Zhuque-3.
nodes.current_reality: One of China’s few truly operational next-gen commercial liquid launchers.
nodes.cost_thesis: Not the end-state economics, but a critical stepping stone that de-risks LandSpace’s reusable future.
nodes.valuation: Common market marks range roughly from RMB 11.8B to RMB 22B depending on reference transaction
nodes.funding: Multiple large rounds plus state-industry fund support; IPO process underway.
nodes.investors: Matrix Partners China, HongShan/Sequoia China, Country Garden Ventures, Yizhuang Industrial Fund and national industry/manufacturing funds.

nodes.route_summary: China’s strongest private reusable methalox contender so far, targeting constellation-scale deployment.
nodes.current_reality: A very important option on the Chinese cost curve, but not yet dependable 2026 supply in a conservative investor base case.
nodes.cost_thesis: If rapid reuse and production mature, Zhuque-3 could become one of the two or three most important Chinese cost leaders by 2030.
nodes.funding: See LandSpace company funding context.
nodes.investors: See LandSpace company investor context.

sources.label: CNSA note on Zhuque-3 entry to orbit

nodes.route_summary: China’s first private liquid launcher to reach orbit, providing a practical bridge to Tianlong-3.
nodes.current_reality: Operational proof matters more than raw payload here: it shows Space Pioneer can close a launch loop, not just build hardware.
nodes.cost_thesis: Economics matter, but Tianlong-2 is most valuable as a credibility bridge to the much more consequential Tianlong-3.
nodes.funding: Cumulative funding reportedly >RMB 4B, including nearly RMB 2.5B added in 2025.
nodes.investors: Bohai / Boyu Capital-related backers, CICC Capital, state asset managers and regional industry funds.

nodes.route_summary: Falcon-9-class Chinese commercial launcher targeting high-frequency constellation deployment with reusable first stage.
nodes.current_reality: High strategic relevance, but not yet dependable live supply in 2026 base-case accounting.
nodes.cost_thesis: One of China’s best shots at a genuinely high-throughput, lower-cost reusable launch business if cadence and manufacturing materialize.
nodes.funding: Near-RMB 2.5B added in 2025 across Pre-D and D; cumulative funding reportedly >RMB 4B.
nodes.investors: National/regional asset managers and industry funds, including Guoyu Gaohua, Jinan/Jigang-related capital, Dongfang Asset and others.

nodes.route_summary: Solid-fueled micro-launch fleet that gives Galactic Energy real market credibility today while larger reusable vehicles mature.
nodes.current_reality: One of China’s most proven private launch businesses by actual delivery count, not just technical ambition.
nodes.cost_thesis: Economically a niche product; strategically its main value is the operating base and customer trust it gives Galactic Energy before Pallas scales.
nodes.funding: >RMB 5B cumulative financing reported by late 2025.
nodes.investors: Beijing commercial-space fund, Nanjing innovation capital, Sichuan industry funds and other Chinese institutional backers.

nodes.route_summary: Reusable liquid launcher aimed at the large-constellation market, using Galactic Energy’s expanding propulsion base.
nodes.current_reality: A major strategic pivot from solid micro-launch into the more economically relevant reusable medium market.
nodes.cost_thesis: If it works, Galactic Energy shifts from ‘reliable micro-launcher’ to ‘real reusable contender’ — a much higher-value position.
nodes.funding: See Galactic Energy company funding context.
nodes.investors: See Galactic Energy company investor context.

sources.label: Pallas test status / 2026 recovery validation plan

nodes.route_summary: China’s most industrialized commercial solid launcher at the higher end of the small/medium segment.
nodes.current_reality: A serious real-delivery node today, with meaningful payload mass already carried.
nodes.cost_thesis: Strong near-term business utility, but long-run margin pressure should rise as reusable liquids scale.
nodes.funding: 12 financing rounds before IPO counseling; mixed ownership with industrial and financial capital.
nodes.investors: Guangzhou Industrial Investment, CICC-related capital, Yuexiu industry funds and other institutional backers.

nodes.route_summary: Large reusable liquid launcher intended to move CAS Space from solid-launch strength into mainstream heavy reusable economics.
nodes.current_reality: The important thing is not just size but that CAS Space openly frames cost coverage and scale efficiency as core commercial constraints.
nodes.cost_thesis: A credible contender if the company can translate institutional backing and existing execution into reusable heavy operations.
nodes.funding: See CAS Space company financing context.
nodes.investors: See CAS Space company investor context.

nodes.route_summary: A uniquely large commercial solid launcher, useful for constellation deployment and time-to-market.
nodes.current_reality: A genuine present-day launch product, not just a future promise, and unusually capable for a commercial solid system.
nodes.cost_thesis: Good near-term economics versus small rockets; long-term structural disadvantage versus reusable liquids remains.
nodes.funding: Early rounds plus B round lifted disclosed financing into the multi-billion RMB range.
nodes.investors: Early Chinese venture and industry capital; multiple institutions across angel, Pre-A, A and B rounds.

nodes.route_summary: Orienspace’s shift from big solid to reusable liquid heavy lift.
nodes.current_reality: This is the real long-term economic bet, while Gravity-1 buys time and credibility.
nodes.cost_thesis: If Gravity-2 reaches service, Orienspace shifts into the market tier where long-run cost and cadence start to matter most.
nodes.funding: See Orienspace company financing context.
nodes.investors: See Orienspace company investor context.

nodes.route_summary: Methalox reusable launcher with ‘first-flight plus sea recovery’ ambition.
nodes.current_reality: Capital just arrived in size; execution is now the whole story.
nodes.cost_thesis: If Hyperbola-3 succeeds technically, the financing gives Interstellar Glory enough room to re-enter the top Chinese challenger set.
nodes.valuation: Public marks vary; roughly RMB 9B-15B range appears in market commentary
nodes.funding: RMB 5.037B D++ round in February 2026, the largest single private Chinese rocket round reported to date.
nodes.investors: Tongchuang Weiye, Jingming Capital, Ganquan Capital, Chengdu industry funds and other new/old backers.

nodes.route_summary: Small reusable liquid launcher focused on vertical recovery and repeat-use economics.
nodes.current_reality: Interesting technology path, but still needs to prove orbital insertion and operational recovery in one programmatic motion.
nodes.cost_thesis: Could matter in a responsive mid-small niche if recovery becomes routine; still far from a bankable supply node.
nodes.funding: Near-RMB 500M new round disclosed in 2025.
nodes.investors: Led by Taian industrial fund / local industry capital in the latest round.

companies.investors: Historical disclosed investors include Google and Fidelity; deep secondary-market demand.
